title: The Inner Equation for Generalized Standard Maps
speaker: Pau Martin
affiliation: Universitat Poliecnica de Catalunya, Barcelona, Spain
abstract: We study particular solutions of the "inner equation" associated to the splitting of separatrices on "generalized standard maps". An exponentially small complete expression for their difference is obtained. We also provide numerical evidence that the inner equation provides quantitative information of the splitting of separatrices even in the case when the limit flow does not predict correctly its asymptotic behavior.

proof_stages.label: Generalized standard maps and natural parametrizations
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the generalized standard maps, imposes analyticity and weak hyperbolicity with h as the logarithmic Lyapunov exponent, notes area preservation, and recalls natural stable/unstable parametrizations conjugating the map to translation by h.

proof_stages.label: Outer Hamiltonian limit with a homoclinic loop
proof_stages.summary: After rescaling variables the map is close to the identity, its first h-order behavior is the time-h map of a one-degree-of-freedom Hamiltonian flow, and an assumed homoclinic loop of this limit flow gives the outer approximation to the invariant manifolds; the same limit is recovered from the scalar invariance equation.

proof_stages.label: Beyond-all-orders splitting and exponential upper bound
proof_stages.summary: The formal h-expansions of the stable and unstable parametrizations agree to every algebraic order, while analyticity of the homoclinic loop in a strip of width σ0 yields the classical exponentially small upper bound of order exp(-2πσ/h).

proof_stages.label: Symplectic measures of the splitting
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes invariant ways to quantify the splitting, especially the lobe area and the Lazutkin invariant given by the determinant of the tangent vectors of the natural stable and unstable parametrizations.

proof_stages.label: Known computations and failure of the naive outer prediction
proof_stages.summary: Known rigorous and numerical cases show how complex singularities of the limit homoclinic often predict the exponential scale, but a polynomial example with the same outer Duffing limit exhibits a much larger splitting, indicating that the leading term must be computed by a different inner analysis.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial hypotheses and inner scaling
proof_stages.summary: Under an additional polynomial-degree hypothesis on the h-expansion of f, the scalar invariance equation is rescaled by a critical power of h so that the h→0 limit is not a differential equation but a limiting difference equation, the inner equation.

proof_stages.label: Sectorial inner solutions and Stokes-type difference formula
proof_stages.summary: The inner equation has stable and unstable sectorial solutions in complex domains, and their difference is given exactly by an exponentially small Fourier/Stokes-type expression with controlled functions.

proof_stages.label: Matching inner solutions to the original manifolds
proof_stages.summary: By matching the original invariant-manifold parametrizations to the inner stable and unstable solutions in the relevant complex regions, the Stokes formula supplies the leading splitting constants and the numerical coefficients in the Lazutkin invariant.

examples.name: Chirikov standard map
examples.note: The standard map, whose limit flow is the pendulum with closest complex homoclinic singularities at iπ/2, illustrates a case where the exponential scale predicted by the analytic-strip theorem is realized and the Lazutkin invariant has a known asymptotic formula.
examples.engagement_reason: the Chirikov standard map is specialized, its pendulum limit flow and complex singularities are identified, and an asymptotic Lasutkin-invariant formula is given

passes.reason: The Chirikov standard map is used as the baseline sinusoidal standard-map case whose splitting formula illustrates the theory, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Macmillan map and perturbations
examples.note: The integrable Macmillan map and its analytic perturbations illustrate another completed splitting computation, with a Duffing limit flow and a leading term expressed through the Borel transform of the perturbation.
examples.engagement_reason: the Macmillan map and its perturbations are discussed through integrability, Duffing limit flow, singularity locations, and a Borel-transform-based splitting asymptotic

passes.reason: The unperturbed Macmillan map is integrable with coincident separatrices and zero splitting, a collapsed base case, while its perturbations are used constructively.

examples.name: Polynomial generalized standard maps in numerical experiments
examples.note: For polynomial f of degree n, numerical formulae show the exponential rate governed by the nearest complex singularity of the limit-flow homoclinic and possible periodic or quasiperiodic oscillatory factors.
examples.engagement_reason: polynomial generalized standard maps are treated via numerical experiments yielding a degree-dependent asymptotic splitting formula and discussion of singularity-driven oscillatory factors

examples.name: Academic polynomial map with Duffing limit but larger splitting
examples.note: A specially chosen polynomial generalized standard map has the same Duffing limit-flow singularities as the Macmillan case but a much larger numerically observed Lazutkin invariant, visibly motivating the move from the outer limit flow to the inner equation.
examples.engagement_reason: the academic polynomial map is concretely analyzed by identifying its Duffing limit, comparing the naive prediction, and reporting numerical computation of a larger exponential splitting correction
